name: Actinium
name_origin: From the Greek 'aktinos' meaning 'ray' or 'beam', referring to its radioactive emissions.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Extremely rare in nature; found in trace amounts in uranium ores at roughly 5 × 10^-15 g per gram of uranium. Most actinium is produced artificially by neutron irradiation of radium-226.

compounds.name: Actinium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: White solid; the most stable oxide of actinium and isostructural with lanthanum oxide.

compounds.name: Actinium(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: Insoluble white solid used in early purification schemes for actinium isotopes.

compounds.name: Actinium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Hygroscopic white solid; intermediate in the preparation of actinium metal by reduction with alkali metals.

uses: Actinium-225 is used in targeted alpha therapy for treating cancers including prostate cancer and leukemia
Actinium-227 mixed with beryllium serves as a neutron source
Used in radioisotope thermoelectric generators in research applications
reactivity_summary: A silvery, highly reactive metal that glows pale blue in the dark from intense self-radiation. It reacts with water and oxygen, and its chemistry resembles that of lanthanum, predominantly forming Ac(III) compounds.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Actinium-227 (half-life 21.77 years) is the longest-lived and is found in nature as a member of the uranium-235 decay series. Actinium-225 (half-life 10 days) is medically important.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
U.S. Department of Energy - Isotope Program: Actinium-225
